target.name: Q-Factor
target.founder: Nir Davidson, Ofer Firstenberg, Yoav Sagi, Guy Raz

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $24M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing

data.narrative:
Q-Factor is a Seed-stage quantum computing company that has raised $24M to date. The broader quantum computing sector includes 3 public companies (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private peers including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

Given Q-Factor's early stage and nascent funding profile, only one valuation method proved applicable. The Stage-Progression approach uses the $24M raised as a proxy for current enterprise value and applies a 2.5x multiplier typical of Seed-to-Series A step-ups in this sector, yielding an implied valuation of $60M. A Peer-Median approach could not be applied because the quantum computing peer set, while containing 7 same-sector companies, lacks sufficient comparables at the same stage or adjacent valuation tier—at least 3 proximate datapoints are required for meaningful triangulation.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $51M (low) to $69M (high), centered on $60M. This range reflects conservative and elevated interpretations of the stage-progression multiplier absent corroborating peer data. Key uncertainties include the scarcity of Seed-stage quantum computing exit data, the heterogeneity of technology approaches across the peer set (which limits direct comparability), and the absence of sufficient same-stage private company valuations to validate the assumed step-up multiple.
